transcript: About 1.7 million Americans earn at or below the federal minimum wage. That's $7.25 per hour, which works out to about $15,000 a year. Even though the cost of living continues to rise, this number has stayed the same for over a decade. Minimum wage is the lowest amount an employer can legally pay an hourly worker, but there are some exceptions. For example, according to federal law, workers who receive tips like waiters and bartenders, employers only have to pay them $2.13 an hour. Disabled workers can earn as little as $1 an hour. But many states have their own laws. For example, 29 states plus Washington, D.C. have a higher minimum wage. You'll always get the highest wage possible. So in states with no minimum wage or a lower one, the federal minimum applies. This amount hasn't changed since July 2009, the longest period in history without a raise. When President Franklin D. Roosevelt first introduced the federal minimum wage, he said it was meant to be the wages of a decent living. In 1938, that was established at 25 cents an hour. Since then, it's been raised 22 times. But after 1968, it just couldn't keep up with inflation. So in 2019, $7.25 was actually only worth $6. People have been pushing for an increase in minimum wage for years, but there's long been debate about how that would affect the overall economy. One side says says if workers are paid more, employers will hire fewer of them, leading to greater unemployment. They also argue that it would cut into the employers' profits and increase inflation. The other side says higher pay would reduce employee turnover, increase consumer spending, help people keep up with the rising cost of living.
